paragraphs.body: I started having sleeping problems during highschool. My problems started with taking forever to fall asleep. And when I finally fell asleep, I felt like I was tossing and turning throughout the night, and I ended up waking up not feeling rested. Then, as my sleeping patterns continued deteriorating, I would wake up in the middle of the night feeling fully awake ready to start my day. For a long time, I did not know that I had a problem. I thought my sleeping patterns were unusual, but I thought that that was part of human diversity. We all have different lifestyles, so I assumed that the way we slept was also unique to each of us. I only realized that my distinctive sleep patterns were a problem when my fatigue accumulated to the point that I felt exhausted practically all the time. And because I was sleep-deprived, I was also increasingly stressed and anxious, which made it even more difficult for me to fall asleep. I learned that I had insomnia and that I needed help. I watched podcasts by sleep experts with or without advanced degrees and tried most of their recommendations. None of them helped, even a little. Much to my surprise, the solution was a little star shaped night light that my parents kept from the years when I was a small child. Miraculously, we still had it and it still worked. And even more miraculously, it helped me fall asleep and to sleep through the night.

paragraphs.body: Insomnia is a curious condition. I don’t think that there is an agreed upon definition of this form of suffering that would be included in reliable sources of knowledge such as the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM). Somnologists who study sleep and circadian rhythms seem to agree that we are supposed to sleep eight uninterrupted hours every night. They emphasize the importance of good sleep routines and seem to be in favor of sleeping aids, medications and supplements that produce a sense of drowsiness or help people stay asleep. They seem to enjoy arguing with each other about the advantages and disadvantages of medications versus supplements, or that of melatonin, benzodiazepines, and antihistamines. I was always suspicious of their learned or improvised wisdom. These experts made their living from people’s suffering and they seemed to have an unsettlingly rigid idea of what constitutes normal sleep. I also found it annoying how they insisted on the importance of sleep and seemed unyielding in rejecting diversity in sleep habits. Most of all, I hated that they just made me feel guilty and, therefore, they ended up intensifying my stress. I felt these experts were gaslighting me, telling me that I was inadequate as I could not even get such a simple everyday practice as sleeping right.

annotations.comment: Keep your verb tense consistent throughout the paragraph. You started in present tense, so stay in present tense :)

annotations.text: They seem to enjoy arguing with each other about the advantages and disadvantages of medications versus supplements, or that of melatonin, benzodiazepines, and antihistamines.
annotations.comment: Great voice and tone. This sentence made me smile.

paragraphs.body: Insomnia, to me, is a metaphor for an uncritical and slavish attachment to productivism that does not have an outside in advanced capitalist societies like ours. In the world of sleep scientists, good sleep is key to maximizing one’s productivity, so sleep is not the outside of productivism, but an essential part of it. Given this understanding of insomnia, which is my understanding, I did not trust sleep science. However, I was exhausted, so I decided to try some of the advice these experts offered. I tried the supplements and medicines they recommended to no avail. I also developed rigorous sleep routines. Every day, I went to bed at the exact same time. My dad did some research and got the darkest curtains available on the market. We also moved my bed around in my room. After I exhausted all the expert advice that I thought might help me and drove my mother crazy, asking her to buy supplement after supplement for me, I started experimenting with my own sleep routines. I tried falling asleep while watching videos on my phone. However, I learned that the blue light coming from my phone kept my brain going and worked against drowsiness. So, clearly, the phone was not a good idea. Then I left the ceiling light on thinking that it could be the darkness that I found stressful as its message was that I must sleep. I get uneasy with things I “must” do. The ceiling light was a no go. Its effect was the opposite of relaxing. Other techniques such as breathing and meditating seemed smart at first, but I got bored with them quickly, so I failed to stick with that routine. Closing my eyes and trying to meditate only brought my anxieties into sharper focus, so I cycled back to my phone to get distracted. But then I had the blue light problem coming into a full circle. Closing my eyes and pushing all sorts of random thoughts away seemed like a good strategy, but my anxieties never failed to come with me to bed to keep me awake.

annotations.text: an uncritical and slavish attachment to productivism that does not have an outside in advanced capitalist societies like ours.
annotations.comment: I'm interested! Also, this idea could be the beginning of a definition paragraph, which you could develop to more intentionally, fully, and clearly define what _you_ mean by a slavish attachment to productivism.

annotations.text: I get uneasy with things I “must” do.
did not trust sleep science. However, I was exhausted, so I decided to try some of the advice these experts offered.

paragraphs.body: Insomnia became a vicious cycle. The more exhausted I was, the worse my anxieties became, and the more anxious I was, the less I was able to sleep. Insomnia is not a matter of bad sleep habits. It is a symptom of mild or severe mental health problems, as well as excessive stress that most of us cannot escape or cannot meditate out of our lives. Sleep routines might help somewhat, but solving the underlying mental health problems takes years. And during those years, we acquire nefarious sleep habits that will take us some more years to correct and recover from. Anxiety can be paralyzing because it is irrational. And anxiety’s favorite time of the day is bedtime. That is when it wants my company, when it asks me to clarify what our relationship really is about. I mean the relationship between anxiety and I. It is around bedtime when I start questioning why I am anxious to begin with. Most recently, whether my anxieties had to do with me starting a new phase of my life in college. Was it because I knew it was going to be challenging for me to make new friends? Was I stressed about the assignments I still had to make up? All I knew was that pondering about my anxieties would just continue keeping me up. Something had to be done.

annotations.text: That is when it wants my company, when it asks me to clarify what our relationship really is about.

paragraphs.body: After trying everything I could think of to fall asleep and not finding a solution, I started thinking about why I did not have insomnia when I was a child. I started thinking how I could recreate the sleep conditions and my bedroom I had as a child, short of installing a crib mobile above my bed. I recalled that I used to sleep with night lights when I was small. Much to my surprise, my dad found the night light that was my favorite. It is a green star shaped light with a warm orange light. I did not pin high hopes on it, but I thought I would give it a try. That inexpensive little night light worked wonders. I don’t know if the solution was the night light or this particular night light, but I think the latter. It will take me a while to understand how this simple little night light my parents got from Target over seventeen years ago helped ease my sleeping problems so significantly. I read somewhere that the famous psychoanalyst, Sigmund Freud, said that “sometimes a cigar is just a cigar.” The most self-evident interpretation of my story with my night light might just be that it transports me back to my childhood when I had a lot fewer things to worry about. It takes me back to a time when my mom read one of those Jack and Annie, or Frog and Toad books to me, and stayed with me until I fell asleep. The night light takes me out of the hamster wheel of productivism and makes me feel loved and supported, no matter what.

annotations.text: hamster wheel of productivism and makes me feel loved and supported, no matter what.
